[2018-01-31 17:08] supergreyman (Greyman): Noobie question: Do you use FF during early-midgame over Walls, even when you have resources to make Walls?
[2018-01-31 17:09] awaclus: I think you almost certainly want the wall if you don't have an absorber yet or if you can't do anything more useful with the blue
[2018-01-31 17:10] supergreyman (Greyman): Like for example, if 5 damage is coming at me, should I defend with FF, Engineer, then Wall? Or just defend with double walls?
[2018-01-31 17:11] awaclus: FF+engineer basically costs 5 (2 for the engineer, 3 for the drone) and an energy and a green, so about as much as a Wall except if you can use the blue, it's better to use the blue because you can save the green
[2018-01-31 17:11] supergreyman (Greyman): Some players say that the cheap cost of making FF allows you to ramp up more damage with the leftover gold.
[2018-01-31 17:12] awaclus: because if the drone is essential, then the FF is not actually very cheap
[2018-01-31 17:13] awaclus: but if you need a bit of tempo in the short term, you can get that by going for FF
[2018-01-31 17:13] supergreyman (Greyman): I guess in a long run, if it doesn't accumulate enough gold, it's generally a bad decision?
[2018-01-31 17:14] supergreyman (Greyman): Unless you need to make a certain good unit?
[2018-01-31 17:16] awaclus: it's also worth noting that FF+E is more flexible than a wall, so if you can save a damage by doing that, that's a point in its favor
[2018-01-31 17:17] supergreyman (Greyman): And I guess during the later part of midgame, where damage is pretty high, FF is more favorable?
[2018-01-31 17:17] awaclus: yeah, because it gives you a short term advantage, and there is no long term at that point
[2018-01-31 17:17] p0lari (Polari): judging when it's correct to F is pretty hard
[2018-01-31 17:18] p0lari (Polari): as a rule of thumb don't buy them unless you NEED them to defend or buying them lets you buy something really good
[2018-01-31 17:18] p0lari (Polari): like getting F to enable spending B on a hellhound instead of a wall is pretty legit
[2018-01-31 17:19] supergreyman (Greyman): Because sometimes, if I were to keep defending with just Walls, my opponent can still keep making some damage while defending. Meanwhile, I spent all resources on just defending.
[2018-01-31 17:25] p0lari (Polari): again the question is what else are they getting by buying F
[2018-01-31 17:25] p0lari (Polari): there's no way sacrificing two drones to buy able to buy a steelsplitter is worth
[2018-01-31 17:26] supergreyman (Greyman): Yeah, it depends a lot on the circumstances.
[2018-01-31 17:27] supergreyman (Greyman): Don't have a replay with me unfortunately. Not on my computer.

[2018-01-31 19:00] mtanzer: forcefield and engineer are worse defenders than wall but they're not terrible by comparison or anything.  if you're facing 5 damage and already have an engineer and a wall I'd still buy wall as a default (keeping the engi alive for granularity is useful anyway), but if it's easier to buy attack with the blue than the green then I'd forcefield.  don't be afraid to forcefield in the early/mid game if it's convenient and wall isn't and definitely do use it if wall is likely to get exploited and forcefield isn't.  one point against using forcefields is in spots where you are or will be overteched, in these cases forcefield is worse than usual.

[2018-02-06 00:06] rachenite: Could someone please tell or link to what inflation is?
[2018-02-06 00:11] crash_overlord: Kinda like how much is 1 gold this turn worth next turn
[2018-02-06 00:11] crash_overlord: So spending 3 gold this turn gives you 1 gold next turn meaning inflation is ~1.33 because 1 gold now is about 1.33 next turn
[2018-02-06 00:11] crash_overlord: if we look at drones
[2018-02-06 00:11] crash_overlord: if the unit you're buying is more cost efficient
[2018-02-06 00:11] mrguy888: Units produce resources so resources now can be invested and are worth more than the same amount of resources in a future turn.
[2018-02-06 00:11] crash_overlord: the inflation goes up
[2018-02-06 00:14] awaclus: so basically it's like real world inflation except the cost of the units doesn't increase
[2018-02-06 00:16] 307th: one way to think about it is to ask which you'd rather have
[2018-02-06 00:16] crash_overlord: You don't actually need to get into or understand any of the specific inflation thingies btw
[2018-02-06 00:16] 307th: +1 Drone on turn 1 or +2 Drones on turn 4
[2018-02-06 00:16] crash_overlord: It's just a really helpful "tool" for analyzing the game
[2018-02-06 00:16] 307th: as it turns out, +1 Drone on turn 1 is better
[2018-02-06 00:17] 307th: because there's so much units that are investments in Prismata
[2018-02-06 00:17] apooche: Quick, someone contrive a situation where +2 drones on turn 4 is better

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: And op units exacerbate the player advantage

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: half a turn early is big

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: idk, some sets are player favoured but still very winnable for the disadvantaged player

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: personally those sets don't bother me

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: it's only a problem if the disadvantaged player is at a really big disadvantage, and can't win, or almost can't win

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: but that tends to be really rare

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: and a lot of the time when people say they couldn't win they're wrong

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: This was probably before your time 307th

messages.author: gex__
messages.content: Grandpa Crash :Drone:

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: but there were a few times where a player (that was quite good) stated he had no way to win in a given set, then got proven wrong by someone else by playing the set a few times. And then the whole ordeal repeated. And then it repeated again. And again.

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: Ah I miss the old days of old cfuge

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: for ways to improve, i think there are a few things you can do

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: analyzing sets is good, watching streams is good

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: when watching streams the best way to improve is to think about how you would play a set

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: in that sense watching vods can be nice because you can pause at the start of the game and think about what strategy you would go for

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: then you can see what the top player does

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: another thing is, when playing, it can be useful to try to force yourself to think about what you'll do on your next turn

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: especially in the opening

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: there's a gold indicator which tells you how much gold you'll get next turn, so turn that on in options if you haven't already

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: Ya know I actually thing for the general person the best way to learn is just to play the game. Playing the game is fun while "learning" might not be :Drone:

messages.author: apooche
messages.content: 307th I don't think "Prismata has a much higher rate of inflation than Starcraft" is a valid statement because you measure them in different units. Prismata should be in inverse turns while Starcraft would be inverse time

messages.author: 307th
messages.content: apooche, my point is that building a Wall that doesn't do anything for a turn can you lose you the game, while building a Bunker that doesn't do anything for a while can be OK

messages.author: crash_overlord
messages.content: As I said earlier, I'm pretty sure if you do that in a gm vs gm game you're screwed

[2018-02-06 03:56] apooche: "and that's kinda my point, there tends to be a line between 'economy stuff' and 'not economy stuff' in starcraft, whereas in prismata everything ends up being an investment" - I don't really see this. If I buy an attacker and harass my opponent's economy with it, that is producing value in a pretty similar way to a Prismata attacker. Attackers trade a lot more in most RTS games obviously, but that is still like a resource trade in mata. Defensive buildings or units that stop my opponent's attackers from harassing my economy are also producing value. How much value you can get off of a particular unit of course depends heavily on the state of the map, but you still have to weigh the value produced by different units and techs vs cost to decide what is best.
[2018-02-06 03:58] apooche: For example if zerglings killed drones slower than the equivalent cost in drones and the town center or whatever it is called produce drones, nobody would ever buy zerglings.
[2018-02-06 03:59] mrguy888: I think by "not economy stuff" he means stuff like noticing the army you sent over to your opponent's base is passing an enemy army and reacting quicker or better microing on two different fronts without letting your production slip.

[2018-02-12 05:57] supergreyman (Greyman): Hey @Msven, I've watched (and rewatched) your video series, and I really like it! It really shows how deep and complicated Prismata can be, and you're able to deliver the message in a pretty clear and understandable way with thorough explanation and great examples, while also not overburdening with too many minor details (which could drag the discussion  for too long). Videos that are concise and easy to understand seem to work very well here.

I especially appreciate the number of examples you show in your videos. For example, on your 3rd video, I like the example of how P2 Chrono Filter+Wincer can be devastating, while providing quick commentary on "what-if" scenarios if P1 attempts to go for early Wincer or late Wincer. As you've already realized, it's pretty difficult to understand abstract ideas without being able to see how they work in front of our eyes.

Speaking of "what-if", in your first video, you've been talking about green-blue strategy and blue-red strategy. Why not make a strategy involving all 3 colors? Would the jack-of-all-trade strategy be too weak? That was a minor thought as I was watching this video.

Now the one thing players may stumble on a bit is the amount of set-reading skill required to understand your first 3 videos (especially in base+11). This isn't necessarily a bad thing since it goes to show the depth of this game and would encourage players to start taking seriously on set-reading (as shown in video #1).

The other thing that may seem too advanced for players is learning to observe and take into consideration your opponent's play, as demonstrated in video #6 on disruption. Long-term planning and making gambits are probably the most advanced topics so far. That probably puts video #8 (managing defensive supply) in a weird position, which might make more sense if placed right after video #4 (long-term investments).

Thanks a lot for your effort! Looking forward to your Unit Study video! 😃
